content:
- Project Mississippi Office Hours – Digital Twin Structured Summary

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- This check-in focused on critical operational and compliance aspects of Project Mississippi, specifically for the Optum partnership. The meeting brought together Zeta leadership and cross-functional team members to align on certification requirements, project financial modeling, and deployment progress of production and non-production zones. Cost implications (notably AWS costing), certification reporting needs (PCI DSS, SOC 2), and the sequence of infrastructure setup dominated the early agenda. The session moved from cost/compliance clarifications to tactical blockers, updates on SaaS/cluster rollouts, and complex dependencies for product configurations. Much of the conversation saw Ramki providing direction on compliance stances, pushing for pragmatic, minimally disruptive solutions, and clarifying ownership and methodology for estimations and operational transitions. Toward the end, there was a focus on data security boundaries (e.g., card flows), required “no human touch” postures for sensitive environments, and expected delivery timelines.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Compliance Reporting Stance Clarified: Ramki reinforced Zeta's position that custom SDK/account-specific details should not appear in audit reports; generic attestation suffices. ("Why would we put any account numbers in an AOC?")
- SOC 2/PCI DSS Audit Cycle Aligned: Agreement that changes introduced for Optum will fall within the regular audit/change management cycles, not requiring exceptional efforts unless audit/applicant insists further.
- AWS Cost Reporting Approach Fixed: Ramki determined that AWS costing will be handled as a pass-through expense and not a committed line item in the SoW, with only indicative estimates provided where required for budgeting.
- Zone Setup Blockers Escalated: Production zone setup for Optum faces AWS quota/infra hurdles, attributed largely to limits on new AWS accounts. Action is underway with AWS/Tarun/Sachin, awaiting further escalation as needed.
- SaaS and Product Onboarding Dependencies Exposed: Critical-path delays stem from ITP SaaS cluster readiness; Abhilash highlighted compounded delays due to cross-team dependencies, with explicit risk on timelines for April and onward.
- Manual vs. Automated Setup Philosophy Reasserted: Ramki reiterated his stance that production/prod zone setups must be reproducible from code (Git), with no manual/human touch, irrespective of “how” it is achieved technically.
- Card Data Isolation Principle Restated: No exposure of card data should occur via Zeta systems/interfaces to customers; demonstration of this separation is to be maintained and articulated to stakeholders.
- Timeline Ownership and Phasing Established: Optum US zone will be provisioned for certification/testing by July, but actual business use (“production enrollment”) is anticipated by October, allowing for a gradual migration and extended overlap of Mumbai and US zones.

- V. Ramki's Insights

1. Certification & Audit Philosophy: Ramki was firm and clear that Zeta attestation letters/AOC should not be tailored for individual customer requests (like displaying specific SDK/account numbers), underlining privacy and standards-practice as the guide. He commented directly that requests for specific additions to audit reports are often from "ill-informed" stakeholders and insisted that the compliance team's position be maintained unless proven technically incorrect. He showed little tolerance for information requests that stray from established compliance boundaries and expects team members to hold the line.

2. Estimation & SoW Costing: On questions around projecting AWS account costs, Ramki insisted no “commitment” be implied or added to contractual SoW for cost elements that are pass-through or variable. He provided clear methodology guidance: use current/reference workloads for best-guess estimates (taking Mumbai/A+ as baseline), and seek inputs from those operating environments (“run” group), not builders. He also repeatedly stressed the difference between internal cost estimation for budgeting and hard SoW commitments.

3. Ownership and Decisiveness: Throughout the dialog, Ramki displayed an assertive, almost brisk leadership style—quick to ask who owns a block or output, and delegating follow-ups without ambiguity. He expects persistent follow-up on pending tasks, as seen when telling Rafi to “never drop” a thread until a concrete answer is received.

4. Stance on Automation, Reproducibility, and Security: Ramki rigidly maintained that production and onboarding procedures (infrastructure, tenants, clusters) must be code-controlled (e.g., via Git) and avoid any “manual” workaround or undocumented human intervention, focusing on audibility and security boundaries over convenience.

5. Incremental Rollout Guidance: He provided clarity on incremental deployments—whatever configuration is ready as of a milestone is to be promoted, with future improvements integrated as they become available, discouraging coupling or blocking initial production on future features or configurations.

6. Data Access and Separation of Responsibilities: Ramki insisted that role boundary demos and proofs are central; if there is no technical ability for third parties to access sensitive card data or SDK elements via Zeta's systems, that suffices. He discouraged engaging in what he views as over-justification or hypothetical compliance concerns.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. PCI/SOC Audit Compliance Communication
- Task: Stick to the established compliance attestation contents when sharing reports with customers; do not accommodate customer-requested additions outside best practice.
- Assigned To: A V S Prabhakar, Compliance Team
- Ramki's Instructions: Maintain a position aligned to industry standards; only discuss adding details if there's a legitimate audit requirement. Escalate to Ramki in areas of doubt.
- Timeline Mentioned: Immediate; applies to ongoing and pending report-sharing cycles.

2. AWS Cost Estimation Process
- Task: Provide an indicative estimate for AWS account costs using Mumbai as reference, ensuring this information is only provided for internal budgeting (not SoW commitment).
- Assigned To: Ramesh Javvaji, Nanda Kishore Reddy Kathi (“run” group)
- Ramki's Instructions: Use actuals and recent footprints as baseline for projections. Collaborate to select the best-fit person for estimation. Confirm the output is not included in SoW, but as a ballpark for Optum’s budgeting.
- Timeline Mentioned: As soon as possible – output needed for Optum’s budgeting cycle.

3. On-Prem & Vendor SOC Cost Calculation
- Task: Finalize the cost calculations for third-party vendor and internal resource components for SOC compliance, now that SaaS has been removed from the scope.
- Assigned To: Praveen Darshanam (to prepare requirements/capacity), A V S Prabhakar (to coordinate costing), with input from Ramesh, Nanda
- Ramki's Instructions: Complete requirements/capacity projections, collect costs from relevant stakeholders, add vendor costs, and coordinate with Rafi for final collation for Optum.
- Timeline Mentioned: No exact date mentioned, but urgency implied for upcoming vendor/commercial discussions.

4. Progress Update and Unblocking
- Task: Continue persistent coordination to resolve AWS service quota/production zone setup issues and update Ramki on outcome.
- Assigned To: Shaik Mohammed Rafi, Aravind Reddy Nagireddy, with involvement from Tarun/Sachin on AWS escalation
- Ramki's Instructions: Persist in follow-up, never abandon open queries until resolved. Inform Ramki of progress; escalate as required.
- Timeline Mentioned: Ongoing, immediate focus as infra is blocking downstream activities.

5. Clarity on Deployment Sequence and Git Control
- Task: Ensure all zone setup and tenant onboarding (especially for prod environments) is reproducible and auditable through code repositories (Git), with no uncontrolled manual actions.
- Assigned To: Velos and zone setup teams
- Ramki's Instructions: Automation is mandatory for traceability. Document and ratify setup procedures in Git, regardless of manual effort required.
- Timeline Mentioned: Applies for all pending and future setup steps.

6. Card Data Flow Assurance
- Task: Prepare and provide clear documentation/position that demonstrates no card data exposure to Optum or its systems where inappropriate. Only affirm technical facts; do not engage in speculative defense.
- Assigned To: Abhilash Rao, Compliance/Security Team
- Ramki's Instructions: Maintain focus on what is technically demonstrable regarding separation of data; avoid responding to hypotheticals not applicable to Zeta systems.
- Timeline Mentioned: For current audit/data security clarifications ongoing with Optum.

- VII. Jira Ticket

Possible Jira Ticket Creation:

- Scope: AWS cost estimation and documentation for Optum budgeting (task from Action #2 and #3 collectively, as they relate to the same outcome of providing a budgetary estimate rather than a committed deliverable)

- Assignee: Ramesh Javvaji & Nanda Kishore Reddy Kathi (joint ticket)
- Justification: This is a concrete, time-bound deliverable with multiple internal stakeholders needing to produce estimations based on current/expected workloads and coordinate the internal sign-off before info is shared with Optum. The ticket ensures the estimation process is tracked and delivered.
- Other action items (e.g., compliance report stance, card data flow demonstrations, or following up with AWS escalation) are more policy/process adherence or persistent communication threads—not concrete, trackable Jira stories at this time.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

1. Confirm Audit Report Content Alignment: It may be helpful to reconfirm with the compliance/audit team that no nonstandard details (SDK/account-specific information) have inadvertently slipped into prior attestation reports before they are shared with customers.
2. Verify AWS Quota Escalation Path: Given the blocking issue with AWS quotas, recommend confirming whether direct AWS enterprise support engagement has been utilized and if there's a secondary escalation method (contacts or expedited pathways) in case further delays arise.
3. Document Key Deployment Sequencing: Suggest formal documentation (one-pager) of the intended prod/non-prod zone deployment sequence, dependencies, and rollback protocols—aligning all teams, especially if there are handovers pending (e.g., from prod to UAT).
4. Revisit Data Flow Mapping with Optum: Prior to next card data flow review, propose a dedicated mapping session to clearly chart all touchpoints, custom vs. standard interfaces, and to have a prepared demonstrable stance for the next Infosec audit.
5. Schedule Next Certification/Costing Review: Propose booking a follow-up session focused solely on cost trackability (vendor/internal/resources and AWS) to preemptively catch any misalignments before these estimates are communicated further.

- End of structured analysis for Ramki Gaddipati's Digital Twin
title: 2025-03-24_Project Mississippi Checkin - Weekly (8)
